attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: How to migrate from Pages to the App Router
3	nav_title: App Router
4	description: Learn how to upgrade your existing Next.js application from the Pages Router to the App Router.
5	---
6	
7	This guide will help you:
8	
9	- [Update your Next.js application from version 12 to version 13](#nextjs-version)
10	- [Upgrade features that work in both the `pages` and the `app` directories](#upgrading-new-features)
11	- [Incrementally migrate your existing application from `pages` to `app`](#migrating-from-pages-to-app)
12	
13	## Upgrading
14	
15	### Node.js Version
16	
17	The minimum Node.js version is now **v18.17**. See the [Node.js documentation](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/) for more information.
18	
19	### Next.js Version
20	
21	To update to Next.js version 13, run the following command using your preferred package manager:
22	
23	```bash package="pnpm"
24	pnpm add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
25	```
26	
27	```bash package="npm"
28	npm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
29	```
30	
31	```bash package="yarn"
32	yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
33	```
34	
35	```bash package="bun"
36	bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
37	```
38	
39	### ESLint Version
40	
41	If you're using ESLint, you need to upgrade your ESLint version:
42	
43	```bash package="pnpm"
44	pnpm add -D eslint-config-next@latest
45	```
46	
47	```bash package="npm"
48	npm install -D eslint-config-next@latest
49	```
50	
51	```bash package="yarn"
52	yarn add -D eslint-config-next@latest
53	```
54	
55	```bash package="bun"
56	bun add -D eslint-config-next@latest
57	```
58	
59	> **Good to know**: You may need to restart the ESLint server in VS Code for the ESLint changes to take effect. Open the Command Palette (`cmd+shift+p` on Mac; `ctrl+shift+p` on Windows) and search for `ESLint: Restart ESLint Server`.
60	
61	## Next Steps
62	
63	After you've updated, see the following sections for next steps:
64	
65	- [Upgrade new features](#upgrading-new-features): A guide to help you upgrade to new features such as the improved Image and Link Components.
66	- [Migrate from the `pages` to `app` directory](#migrating-from-pages-to-app): A step-by-step guide to help you incrementally migrate from the `pages` to the `app` directory.
67	
68	## Upgrading New Features
69	
70	Next.js 13 introduced the new [App Router](/docs/app) with new features and conventions. The new Router is available in the `app` directory and co-exists with the `pages` directory.
71	
72	Upgrading to Next.js 13 does **not** require using the App Router. You can continue using `pages` with new features that work in both directories, such as the updated [Image component](#image-component), [Link component](#link-component), [Script component](#script-component), and [Font optimization](#font-optimization).
73	
74	### `<Image/>` Component
75	
76	Next.js 12 introduced new improvements to the Image Component with a temporary import: `next/future/image`. These improvements included less client-side JavaScript, easier ways to extend and style images, better accessibility, and native browser lazy loading.
77	
78	In version 13, this new behavior is now the default for `next/image`.
79	
80	There are two codemods to help you migrate to the new Image Component:
81	
82	- [**`next-image-to-legacy-image` codemod**](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#next-image-to-legacy-image): Safely and automatically renames `next/image` imports to `next/legacy/image`. Existing components will maintain the same behavior.
83	- [**`next-image-experimental` codemod**](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#next-image-experimental): Dangerously adds inline styles and removes unused props. This will change the behavior of existing components to match the new defaults. To use this codemod, you need to run the `next-image-to-legacy-image` codemod first.
84	
85	### `<Link>` Component
86	
87	The [`<Link>` Component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) no longer require…(+8508)

1 --- 2 title: Layouts and Pages 3 description: Learn how to create your first pages and layouts, and link between them with the Link component. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout 10 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/page 11 - app/api-reference/components/link 12 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes 13 --- 14 15 Next.js uses **file-system based routing**, meaning you can use folders and files to define routes. This page will guide you through how to create layouts and pages, and link between them. 16 17 ## Creating a page 18 19 A **page** is UI that is rendered on a specific route. To create a page, add a [`page` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) inside the `app` directory and default export a React component. For example, to create an index page (`/`): 20 21 28 29 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 30 export default function Page() { 31 return

Hello Next.js!

32 } 33 ``` 34 35 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 36 export default function Page() { 37 return

Hello Next.js!

38 } 39 ``` 40 41 ## Creating a layout 42 43 A layout is UI that is **shared** between multiple pages. On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender. 44 45 You can define a layout by default exporting a React component from a [`layout` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout). The component should accept a `children` prop which can be a page or another [layout](#nesting-layouts). 46 47 For example, to create a layout that accepts your index page as child, add a `layout` file inside the `app` directory: 48 49 56 57 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 58 export default function DashboardLayout({ 59 children, 60 }: { 61 children: React.ReactNode 62 }) { 63 return ( 64 65 66 {/* Layout UI */} 67 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 68
{children}
69 70 71 ) 72 } 73 ``` 74 75 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 76 export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) { 77 return ( 78 79 80 {/* Layout UI */} 81 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 82
{children}
83 84 85 ) 86 } 87 ``` 88 89 The layout above is called a [root layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout) because it's defined at the root of the `app` directory. The root layout is **required** and must contain `html` and `body` tags. 90 91 ## Creating a nested route 92 93 A nested route is a route composed of multiple URL segments. For example, the `/blog/[slug]` route is composed of three segments: 94 95 - `/` (Root Segment) 96 - `blog` (Segment) 97 - `[slug]` (Leaf Segment) 98 99 In Next.js: 100 101 - **Folders** are used to define the route segments that map to URL segments. 102 - **Files** (like `page` and `layout`) are used to create UI that is shown for a segment. 103 104 To create nested routes, you can nest folders inside each other. For example, to add a route for `/blog`, create a folder called `blog` in the `app` directory. Then, to make `/blog` publicly accessible, add a `page.tsx` file: 105 106

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: layout.js
3	description: API reference for the layout.js file.
4	---
5	
6	The `layout` file is used to define a layout in your Next.js application.
7	
8	```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" switcher
9	export default function DashboardLayout({
10	  children,
11	}: {
12	  children: React.ReactNode
13	}) {
14	  return <section>{children}</section>
15	}
16	```
17	
18	```jsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.js" switcher
19	export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) {
20	  return <section>{children}</section>
21	}
22	```
23	
24	A **root layout** is the top-most layout in the root `app` directory. It is used to define the `<html>` and `<body>` tags and other globally shared UI.
25	
26	```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher
27	export default function RootLayout({
28	  children,
29	}: {
30	  children: React.ReactNode
31	}) {
32	  return (
33	    <html lang="en">
34	      <body>{children}</body>
35	    </html>
36	  )
37	}
38	```
39	
40	```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher
41	export default function RootLayout({ children }) {
42	  return (
43	    <html lang="en">
44	      <body>{children}</body>
45	    </html>
46	  )
47	}
48	```
49	
50	## Reference
51	
52	### Props
53	
54	#### `children` (required)
55	
56	Layout components should accept and use a `children` prop. During rendering, `children` will be populated with the route segments the layout is wrapping. These will primarily be the component of a child [Layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) (if it exists) or [Page](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), but could also be other special files like [Loading](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) or [Error](/docs/app/getting-started/error-handling) when applicable.
57	
58	#### `params` (optional)
59	
60	A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) object from the root segment down to that layout.
61	
62	```tsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.tsx" switcher
63	export default async function Layout({
64	  children,
65	  params,
66	}: {
67	  children: React.ReactNode
68	  params: Promise<{ team: string }>
69	}) {
70	  const { team } = await params
71	}
72	```
73	
74	```jsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js" switcher
75	export default async function Layout({ children, params }) {
76	  const { team } = await params
77	}
78	```
79	
80	| Example Route                     | URL            | `params`                           |
81	| --------------------------------- | -------------- | ---------------------------------- |
82	| `app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js`  | `/dashboard/1` | `Promise<{ team: '1' }>`           |
83	| `app/shop/[tag]/[item]/layout.js` | `/shop/1/2`    | `Promise<{ tag: '1', item: '2' }>` |
84	| `app/blog/[...slug]/layout.js`    | `/blog/1/2`    | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>`    |
85	
86	- Since the `params` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values.
87	  - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future.
88	
89	### Layout Props Helper
90	
91	You can type layouts with `LayoutProps` to get a strongly typed `params` and named slots inferred from your directory structure. `LayoutProps` is a globally available helper.
92	
93	```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx"
94	export default function Layout(props: LayoutProps<'/dashboard'>) {
95	  return (
96	    <section>
97	      {props.children}
98	      {/* If you have app/dashboard/@analytics, it appears as a typed slot: */}
99	      {/* {props.analytics} */}
100	    </section>

1 --- 2 title: not-found.js 3 description: API reference for the not-found.js file. 4 --- 5 6 Next.js provides two conventions to handle not found cases: 7 8 - **`not-found.js`**: Used when you call the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function in a route segment. 9 - **`global-not-found.js`**: Used to define a global 404 page for unmatched routes across your entire app. This is handled at the routing level and doesn't depend on rendering a layout or page. 10 11 ## `not-found.js` 12 13 The **not-found** file is used to render UI when the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function is thrown within a route segment. Along with serving a custom UI, Next.js will return a `200` HTTP status code for streamed responses, and `404` for non-streamed responses. 14 15 ```tsx filename="app/not-found.tsx" switcher 16 import Link from 'next/link' 17 18 export default function NotFound() { 19 return ( 20
21

Not Found

22

Could not find requested resource

23 Return Home 24
25 ) 26 } 27 ``` 28 29 ```jsx filename="app/blog/not-found.js" switcher 30 import Link from 'next/link' 31 32 export default function NotFound() { 33 return ( 34
35

Not Found

36

Could not find requested resource

37 Return Home 38
39 ) 40 } 41 ``` 42 43 ## `global-not-found.js` (experimental) 44 45 The `global-not-found.js` file lets you define a 404 page for your entire application. Unlike `not-found.js`, which works at the route level, this is used when a requested URL doesn't match any route at all. Next.js **skips rendering** and directly returns this global page. 46 47 The `global-not-found.js` file bypasses your app's normal rendering, which means you'll need to import any global styles, fonts, or other dependencies that your 404 page requires. 48 49 > **Good to know**: A smaller version of your global styles, and a simpler font family could improve performance of this page. 50 51 `global-not-found.js` is useful when you can't build a 404 page using a combination of `layout.js` and `not-found.js`. This can happen in two cases: 52 53 - Your app has multiple root layouts (e.g. `app/(admin)/layout.tsx` and `app/(shop)/layout.tsx`), so there's no single layout to compose a global 404 from. 54 - Your root layout is defined using top-level dynamic segments (e.g. `app/[country]/layout.tsx`), which makes composing a consistent 404 page harder. 55 56 To enable it, add the `globalNotFound` flag in `next.config.ts`: 57 58 ```tsx filename="next.config.ts" 59 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 60 61 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 62 experimental: { 63 globalNotFound: true, 64 }, 65 } 66 67 export default nextConfig 68 ``` 69 70 Then, create a file in the root of the `app` directory: `app/global-not-found.js`: 71 72 ```tsx filename="app/global-not-found.tsx" switcher 73 // Import global styles and fonts 74 import './globals.css' 75 import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' 76 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 77 78 const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) 79 80 export const metadata: Metadata = { 81 title: '404 - Page Not Found', 82 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 83 } 84 85 export default function GlobalNotFound() { 86 return ( 87 88 89

404 - Page Not Found

90

This page does not exist.

91 92 93 ) 94 } 95 ``` 96 97 ```jsx filename="app/global-not-found.js" switcher 98 // Import global styles and fonts 99 import './globals.css' 100 import { Inter } from 'next/font/google'

1 --- 2 title: error.js 3 description: API reference for the error.js special file. 4 related: 5 title: Learn more about error handling 6 links: 7 - app/getting-started/error-handling 8 --- 9 10 An **error** file allows you to handle unexpected runtime errors and display fallback UI. 11 12 19 20 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/error.tsx" switcher 21 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 22 23 import { useEffect } from 'react' 24 25 export default function Error({ 26 error, 27 reset, 28 }: { 29 error: Error & { digest?: string } 30 reset: () => void 31 }) { 32 useEffect(() => { 33 // Log the error to an error reporting service 34 console.error(error) 35 }, [error]) 36 37 return ( 38
39

Something went wrong!

40 48
49 ) 50 } 51 ``` 52 53 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/error.js" switcher 54 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 55 56 import { useEffect } from 'react' 57 58 export default function Error({ error, reset }) { 59 useEffect(() => { 60 // Log the error to an error reporting service 61 console.error(error) 62 }, [error]) 63 64 return ( 65
66

Something went wrong!

67 75
76 ) 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 `error.js` wraps a route segment and its nested children in a [React Error Boundary](https://react.dev/reference/react/Component#catching-rendering-errors-with-an-error-boundary). When an error throws within the boundary, the `error` component shows as the fallback UI. 81 82 89 90 > **Good to know**: 91 > 92 > - The [React DevTools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools) allow you to toggle error boundaries to test error states. 93 > - If you want errors to bubble up to the parent error boundary, you can `throw` when rendering the `error` component. 94 95 ## Reference 96 97 ### Props 98 99 #### `error` 100

1 --- 2 title: Fetching Data 3 description: Learn how to fetch data and stream content that depends on data. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/guides/data-security 9 - app/api-reference/functions/fetch 10 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading 11 - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/logging 12 - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/taint 13 --- 14 15 This page will walk you through how you can fetch data in [Server and Client Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components), and how to [stream](#streaming) components that depend on data. 16 17 ## Fetching data 18 19 ### Server Components 20 21 You can fetch data in Server Components using any asynchronous I/O, such as: 22 23 1. The [`fetch` API](#with-the-fetch-api) 24 2. An [ORM or database](#with-an-orm-or-database) 25 3. Reading from the filesystem using Node.js APIs like `fs` 26 27 #### With the `fetch` API 28 29 To fetch data with the `fetch` API, turn your component into an asynchronous function, and await the `fetch` call. For example: 30 31 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 32 export default async function Page() { 33 const data = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/blog') 34 const posts = await data.json() 35 return ( 36
    37 {posts.map((post) => ( 38
  • {post.title}
    • 39 ))} 40
41 ) 42 } 43 ``` 44 45 ```jsx filename="app/blog/page.js" switcher 46 export default async function Page() { 47 const data = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/blog') 48 const posts = await data.json() 49 return ( 50
    51 {posts.map((post) => ( 52
  • {post.title}
    • 53 ))} 54
55 ) 56 } 57 ``` 58 59 > **Good to know:** 60 > 61 > - `fetch` responses are not cached by default. However, Next.js will [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) the route and the output will be cached for improved performance. If you'd like to opt into [dynamic rendering](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), use the `{ cache: 'no-store' }` option. See the [`fetch` API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch). 62 > - During development, you can log `fetch` calls for better visibility and debugging. See the [`logging` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/logging). 63 64 #### With an ORM or database 65 66 Since Server Components are rendered on the server, you can safely make database queries using an ORM or database client. Turn your component into an asynchronous function, and await the call: 67 68 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 69 import { db, posts } from '@/lib/db' 70 71 export default async function Page() { 72 const allPosts = await db.select().from(posts) 73 return ( 74
    75 {allPosts.map((post) => ( 76
  • {post.title}

79 ) 80 } 81 ``` 82 83 ```jsx filename="app/blog/page.js" switcher 84 import { db, posts } from '@/lib/db' 85 86 export default async function Page() { 87 const allPosts = await db.select().from(posts) 88 return ( 89
    90 {allPosts.map((post) => ( 91
  • {post.title}
    • 92 ))} 93
94 ) 95 } 96 ``` 97 98 ### Client Components 99 100 There are two ways to fetch data in Client Components, using: 101 102 1. React's [`use` API](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) 103 2. A community library like [SWR](https://swr.vercel.app/) or [React Query](https://tanstack.com/query/latest) 104 105 #### Streaming data with the `use` API 106 107 You can use React's [`use` API](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) to [stream](#streaming) data from the server to client. Start by fetching data in your Server component, and pass the promise to your Client Component as prop: 108 109 ```tsx filen…(+355)

attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: generateStaticParams
3	description: API reference for the generateStaticParams function.
4	---
5	
6	The `generateStaticParams` function can be used in combination with [dynamic route segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) to [**statically generate**](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) routes at build time instead of on-demand at request time.
7	
8	`generateStaticParams` can be used with:
9	
10	- [Pages](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) (`page.tsx`/`page.js`)
11	- [Layouts](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) (`layout.tsx`/`layout.js`)
12	- [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) (`route.ts`/`route.js`)
13	
14	```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher
15	// Return a list of `params` to populate the [slug] dynamic segment
16	export async function generateStaticParams() {
17	  const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json())
18	
19	  return posts.map((post) => ({
20	    slug: post.slug,
21	  }))
22	}
23	
24	// Multiple versions of this page will be statically generated
25	// using the `params` returned by `generateStaticParams`
26	export default async function Page({
27	  params,
28	}: {
29	  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>
30	}) {
31	  const { slug } = await params
32	  // ...
33	}
34	```
35	
36	```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher
37	// Return a list of `params` to populate the [slug] dynamic segment
38	export async function generateStaticParams() {
39	  const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json())
40	
41	  return posts.map((post) => ({
42	    slug: post.slug,
43	  }))
44	}
45	
46	// Multiple versions of this page will be statically generated
47	// using the `params` returned by `generateStaticParams`
48	export default async function Page({ params }) {
49	  const { slug } = await params
50	  // ...
51	}
52	```
53	
54	> **Good to know**:
55	>
56	> - You can use the [`dynamicParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamicparams) segment config option to control what happens when a dynamic segment is visited that was not generated with `generateStaticParams`.
57	> - You must return [an empty array from `generateStaticParams`](#all-paths-at-build-time) or utilize [`export const dynamic = 'force-static'`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamic) in order to revalidate (ISR) [paths at runtime](#all-paths-at-runtime).
58	> - During `next dev`, `generateStaticParams` will be called when you navigate to a route.
59	> - During `next build`, `generateStaticParams` runs before the corresponding Layouts or Pages are generated.
60	> - During revalidation (ISR), `generateStaticParams` will not be called again.
61	> - `generateStaticParams` replaces the [`getStaticPaths`](/docs/pages/api-reference/functions/get-static-paths) function in the Pages Router.
62	
63	## Parameters
64	
65	`options.params` (optional)
66	
67	If multiple dynamic segments in a route use `generateStaticParams`, the child `generateStaticParams` function is executed once for each set of `params` the parent generates.
68	
69	The `params` object contains the populated `params` from the parent `generateStaticParams`, which can be used to [generate the `params` in a child segment](#multiple-dynamic-segments-in-a-route).
70	
71	## Returns
72	
73	`generateStaticParams` should return an array of objects where each object represents the populated dynamic segments of a single route.
74	
75	- Each property in the object is a dynamic segment to be filled in for the route.
76	- The properties name is the segment's name, and the properties value is what that segment should be filled in with.
77	
78	| Example Route                    | `generateStaticParams` Return Type        |
79	| -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
80	| `/product/[id]`                  | `{ id: string }[]`                        |
81	| `…(+646)

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: Metadata and OG images
3	description: Learn how to add metadata to your pages and create dynamic OG images.
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: Learn more about the Metadata APIs mentioned in this page.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata
9	    - app/api-reference/functions/generate-viewport
10	    - app/api-reference/functions/image-response
11	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata
12	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons
13	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image
14	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots
15	    - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap
16	    - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/htmlLimitedBots
17	---
18	
19	The Metadata APIs can be used to define your application metadata for improved SEO and web shareability and include:
20	
21	1. [The static `metadata` object](#static-metadata)
22	2. [The dynamic `generateMetadata` function](#generated-metadata)
23	3. Special [file conventions](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) that can be used to add static or dynamically generated [favicons](#favicons) and [OG images](#static-open-graph-images).
24	
25	With all the options above, Next.js will automatically generate the relevant `<head>` tags for your page, which can be inspected in the browser's developer tools.
26	
27	The `metadata` object and `generateMetadata` function exports are only supported in Server Components.
28	
29	## Default fields
30	
31	There are two default `meta` tags that are always added even if a route doesn't define metadata:
32	
33	- The [meta charset tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/meta#attr-charset) sets the character encoding for the website.
34	- The [meta viewport tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Viewport_meta_tag) sets the viewport width and scale for the website to adjust for different devices.
35	
36	```html
37	<meta charset="utf-8" />
38	<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
39	```
40	
41	The other metadata fields can be defined with the `Metadata` object (for [static metadata](#static-metadata)) or the `generateMetadata` function (for [generated metadata](#generated-metadata)).
42	
43	## Static metadata
44	
45	To define static metadata, export a [`Metadata` object](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-object) from a static [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) or [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) file. For example, to add a title and description to the blog route:
46	
47	```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher
48	import type { Metadata } from 'next'
49	
50	export const metadata: Metadata = {
51	  title: 'My Blog',
52	  description: '...',
53	}
54	
55	export default function Layout() {}
56	```
57	
58	```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher
59	export const metadata = {
60	  title: 'My Blog',
61	  description: '...',
62	}
63	
64	export default function Layout() {}
65	```
66	
67	You can view a full list of available options, in the [`generateMetadata` documentation](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-fields).
68	
69	## Generated metadata
70	
71	You can use [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) function to `fetch` metadata that depends on data. For example, to fetch the title and description for a specific blog post:
72	
73	```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher
74	import type { Metadata, ResolvingMetadata } from 'next'
75	
76	type Props = {
77	  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>

1	---
2	title: Route Segment Config
3	description: Learn about how to configure options for Next.js route segments.
4	---
5	
6	> **Good to know**:
7	>
8	> - The options outlined on this page are disabled if the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) flag is on, and will eventually be deprecated in the future.
9	> - Route Segment options only take effect in Server Component Pages, Layouts, or Route Handlers.
10	> - `generateStaticParams` cannot be used inside a `'use client'` file.
11	
12	The Route Segment options allows you to configure the behavior of a [Page](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout), [Layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout), or [Route Handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) by directly exporting the following variables:
13	
14	| Option                                | Type                                                                                                                      | Default                    |
15	| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
16	| [`dynamic`](#dynamic)                 | `'auto' \| 'force-dynamic' \| 'error' \| 'force-static'`                                                                  | `'auto'`                   |
17	| [`dynamicParams`](#dynamicparams)     | `boolean`                                                                                                                 | `true`                     |
18	| [`revalidate`](#revalidate)           | `false \| 0 \| number`                                                                                                    | `false`                    |
19	| [`fetchCache`](#fetchcache)           | `'auto' \| 'default-cache' \| 'only-cache' \| 'force-cache' \| 'force-no-store' \| 'default-no-store' \| 'only-no-store'` | `'auto'`                   |
20	| [`runtime`](#runtime)                 | `'nodejs' \| 'edge'`                                                                                                      | `'nodejs'`                 |
21	| [`preferredRegion`](#preferredregion) | `'auto' \| 'global' \| 'home' \| string \| string[]`                                                                      | `'auto'`                   |
22	| [`maxDuration`](#maxduration)         | `number`                                                                                                                  | Set by deployment platform |
23	
24	## Options
25	
26	### `dynamic`
27	
28	Change the dynamic behavior of a layout or page to fully static or fully dynamic.
29	
30	```tsx filename="layout.tsx | page.tsx | route.ts" switcher
31	export const dynamic = 'auto'
32	// 'auto' | 'force-dynamic' | 'error' | 'force-static'
33	```
34	
35	```js filename="layout.js | page.js | route.js" switcher
36	export const dynamic = 'auto'
37	// 'auto' | 'force-dynamic' | 'error' | 'force-static'
38	```
39	
40	> **Good to know**: The new model in the `app` directory favors granular caching control at the `fetch` request level over the binary all-or-nothing model of `getServerSideProps` and `getStaticProps` at the page-level in the `pages` directory. The `dynamic` option is a way to opt back in to the previous model as a convenience and provides a simpler migration path.
41	
42	- **`'auto'`** (default): The default option to cache as much as possible without preventing any components from opting into dynamic behavior.
43	- **`'force-dynamic'`**: Force [dynamic rendering](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), which will result in routes being rendered for each user at request time. This option is equivalent to:
44	  - Setting the option of every `fetch()` request in a layout or page to `{ cache: 'no-store', next: { revalidate: 0 } }`.
45	  - Setting the segment config to `export const fetchCache = 'force-…(+4509)

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: useRouter
3	description: API reference for the useRouter hook.
4	---
5	
6	The `useRouter` hook allows you to programmatically change routes inside [Client Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components).
7	
8	> **Recommendation:** Use the [`<Link>` component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) for navigation unless you have a specific requirement for using `useRouter`.
9	
10	```tsx filename="app/example-client-component.tsx" switcher
11	'use client'
12	
13	import { useRouter } from 'next/navigation'
14	
15	export default function Page() {
16	  const router = useRouter()
17	
18	  return (
19	    <button type="button" onClick={() => router.push('/dashboard')}>
20	      Dashboard
21	    </button>
22	  )
23	}
24	```
25	
26	```jsx filename="app/example-client-component.js" switcher
27	'use client'
28	
29	import { useRouter } from 'next/navigation'
30	
31	export default function Page() {
32	  const router = useRouter()
33	
34	  return (
35	    <button type="button" onClick={() => router.push('/dashboard')}>
36	      Dashboard
37	    </button>
38	  )
39	}
40	```
41	
42	## `useRouter()`
43	
44	- `router.push(href: string, { scroll: boolean })`: Perform a client-side navigation to the provided route. Adds a new entry into the [browser's history stack](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/History_API).
45	- `router.replace(href: string, { scroll: boolean })`: Perform a client-side navigation to the provided route without adding a new entry into the browser’s history stack.
46	- `router.refresh()`: Refresh the current route. Making a new request to the server, re-fetching data requests, and re-rendering Server Components. The client will merge the updated React Server Component payload without losing unaffected client-side React (e.g. `useState`) or browser state (e.g. scroll position).
47	- `router.prefetch(href: string, options?: { onInvalidate?: () => void })`: [Prefetch](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching) the provided route for faster client-side transitions. The optional `onInvalidate` callback is called when the [prefetched data becomes stale](/docs/app/guides/prefetching#extending-or-ejecting-link).
48	- `router.back()`: Navigate back to the previous route in the browser’s history stack.
49	- `router.forward()`: Navigate forwards to the next page in the browser’s history stack.
50	
51	> **Good to know**:
52	>
53	> - You must not send untrusted or unsanitized URLs to `router.push` or `router.replace`, as this can open your site to cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities. For example, `javascript:` URLs sent to `router.push` or `router.replace` will be executed in the context of your page.
54	> - The `<Link>` component automatically prefetch routes as they become visible in the viewport.
55	> - `refresh()` could re-produce the same result if fetch requests are cached. Other Dynamic APIs like `cookies` and `headers` could also change the response.
56	> - The `onInvalidate` callback is called at most once per prefetch request. It signals when you may want to trigger a new prefetch for updated route data.
57	
58	### Migrating from `next/router`
59	
60	- The `useRouter` hook should be imported from `next/navigation` and not `next/router` when using the App Router
61	- The `pathname` string has been removed and is replaced by [`usePathname()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-pathname)
62	- The `query` object has been removed and is replaced by [`useSearchParams()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params)
63	- `router.events` has been replaced. [See below.](#router-events)
64	
65	[View the full migration guide](/docs/app/guides/migrating/app-router-migration).
66	
67	## Examples
68	
69	### Router events
70	
71	You can listen for page changes by composing other Client Component hooks like `usePathname` and `useSearchParams`.
72	
73	```jsx filename="app/components/navigation-events.js"
74	'use client'
75	
76	import { useEffect } from 'react'
77	i…(+141)
